title: Althea Garrison becomes the first transgender state legislator in the US
description: Althea Garrison won election to the Massachusetts House of Representatives in 1992, making her the first transgender person to serve in a US state legislature. Her transgender status was not public knowledge at the time of her election, and she served a single term.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Lesbian Avengers form in New York City
description: Ana María Simo, Sarah Schulman, Maxine Wolfe, Anne-Christine d'Adesky, Marie Honan and Anne Maguire launched the Lesbian Avengers in New York City in 1992. The direct-action group focused on lesbian visibility and survival and later spread to chapters in many cities.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Tokyo hosts an international lesbian and gay film festival
description: The Tokyo International Lesbian & Gay Film Festival took place in Japan in 1992, bringing queer cinema to a broad Japanese audience and becoming a recurring fixture of LGBTQ culture in the country.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: California bars employment discrimination based on sexual orientation
description: Governor Pete Wilson signed legislation in 1992 prohibiting California employers from discriminating against workers because of their sexual orientation. The source names Assembly Bill 101, which Wilson had in fact vetoed in 1991; the protection signed in 1992 came through Assembly Bill 2601.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Deb Price starts the first nationally syndicated column on gay life in US mainstream media
description: Journalist Deb Price began her column in The Detroit News in 1992. Syndicated nationally, it was the first regular column about gay life to run in mainstream American newspapers.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Norway enacts registered partnerships for same-sex couples
description: Norway passed its registered partnership law in 1993 and the law took effect the same year, making it the second country to give same-sex couples a legal partnership status. Adoption rights were excluded until 2009, and the scheme was superseded by full marriage equality in 2009.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Norfolk Island repeals its sodomy law
description: The Australian territory of Norfolk Island removed its sodomy provisions in 1993, ending the criminalization of consensual same-sex acts in the territory.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Ireland decriminalizes same-sex acts
description: Ireland repealed its laws criminalizing consensual sexual acts between men in 1993, ending legislation that dated back to the Victorian era.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Russia decriminalizes male same-sex acts
description: Russia repealed the Soviet-era article criminalizing consensual sex between men in 1993. The change applied nationwide with the exception of the Chechen Republic, according to the source timeline.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Lithuania decriminalizes same-sex acts
description: Lithuania removed the criminal ban on consensual sex between men from its penal code in 1993, part of the wave of decriminalization across post-Soviet states.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Belarus decriminalizes same-sex acts
description: Belarus repealed its criminal ban on consensual sex between men. The source timeline lists the change under 1993, while most references date the repeal to the 1994 revision of the criminal code.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Gibraltar decriminalizes same-sex acts
description: The UK territory of Gibraltar ended its criminal ban on consensual sex between men in 1993.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Minnesota adds gender identity to its anti-discrimination law
description: In 1993 Minnesota amended its Human Rights Act to cover gender identity, becoming the first US state to write transgender protections into statewide anti-discrimination law.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: New Zealand outlaws discrimination based on sexual orientation and HIV status
description: New Zealand's parliament passed the Human Rights Amendment Act in 1993, prohibiting discrimination on the grounds of sexual orientation or HIV status nationwide.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Saskatchewan bans discrimination based on sexual orientation
description: The Canadian province of Saskatchewan added sexual orientation to its human-rights legislation in 1993, protecting residents from discrimination.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: New Zealand ends its ban on gay people in the military
description: New Zealand lifted its prohibition on gay and lesbian personnel serving in the armed forces in 1993, opening military service regardless of sexual orientation.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Brandon Teena is murdered in Nebraska
description: Brandon Teena, a 21-year-old transgender man, was murdered in Humboldt, Nebraska on 31 December 1993 after being outed and assaulted. The case drew national attention to anti-transgender violence and later inspired the film Boys Don't Cry.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Melissa Etheridge comes out as a lesbian
description: Rock musician Melissa Etheridge publicly came out as a lesbian in 1993, becoming one of the most prominent openly gay figures in American popular music.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Amanda Bearse comes out as a lesbian
description: Actress Amanda Bearse, known for the sitcom Married... with Children, came out publicly as a lesbian in 1993, at a time when few working television actors were out.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Triangle Ball marks the first US inaugural ball held in honor of gays and lesbians
description: During the 1993 presidential inauguration festivities in Washington, D.C., the Triangle Ball became the first inaugural ball in US history organized in honor of gay and lesbian Americans.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: 20,000 women march in the first Dyke March
description: The Lesbian Avengers organized the first Dyke March in Washington, D.C. in April 1993, on the eve of the March on Washington for Lesbian, Gay and Bi Equal Rights. Around 20,000 lesbians and their allies took part, launching what became an annual tradition in many cities.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Roberta Achtenberg becomes the first openly gay official confirmed by the US Senate
description: President Bill Clinton appointed Roberta Achtenberg as Assistant Secretary for Fair Housing and Equal Opportunity in 1993. Her Senate confirmation made her the first openly gay or lesbian person nominated by a US president and confirmed to federal office.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Lea DeLaria breaks the late-night barrier for openly gay comedians
description: Comedian Lea DeLaria appeared on The Arsenio Hall Show in 1993, becoming the first openly gay comic to perform on a US late-night talk show.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Comedy Central airs the first all-gay stand-up special, hosted by Lea DeLaria
description: In December 1993 Lea DeLaria hosted Out There on Comedy Central, the first stand-up comedy special made up entirely of gay performers to air on US television.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The US enacts the Don't Ask, Don't Tell military policy
description: In 1993 the United States replaced its outright ban on lesbian, gay and bisexual servicemembers with the Don't Ask, Don't Tell policy, which barred the military from asking about sexual orientation but still discharged personnel who disclosed same-sex orientation or conduct. The policy remained in force until 2011.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Israel begins recognizing unregistered cohabitation for same-sex couples
description: In 1994 Israel started extending unregistered-cohabitation recognition to same-sex couples, granting partner benefits without a formal partnership registry. Adoption remained excluded, with step-adoption following only in 2005.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: South Africa's interim constitution protects against sexual-orientation discrimination
description: South Africa's interim constitution, in force from 1994, included sexual orientation among the grounds protected from discrimination — the first national constitution in the world to do so. The protection carried into the final constitution of 1996.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Bermuda decriminalizes same-sex acts
description: The UK territory of Bermuda ended its criminal ban on consensual sex between men in 1994 through the Stubbs Bill.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Isle of Man decriminalizes same-sex acts
description: The Isle of Man, a UK Crown Dependency, ended its criminal ban on consensual sex between men. The source timeline lists the change under 1994, while the island's Sexual Offences Act is documented as taking effect in 1992.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Serbia decriminalizes male same-sex acts
description: Serbia removed the criminal ban on consensual sex between men from its penal code in 1994.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Australia's federal parliament overrides remaining state sodomy laws
description: The Commonwealth of Australia passed the Human Rights (Sexual Conduct) Act in 1994, establishing a federal right to sexual privacy for adults. The act overrode remaining state-level criminalization, in response to the UN Human Rights Committee's Toonen decision concerning Tasmania.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The UK lowers the age of consent for gay men to 18
description: In 1994 the United Kingdom reduced the age of consent for sex between men from 21 to 18. The change was only a partial equalization, as the age of consent for heterosexual sex remained 16; full parity followed in 2001.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The American Medical Association stops treating homosexuality as an illness
description: In 1994 the American Medical Association removed language classifying homosexuality as an illness from its policies, aligning the organization with the earlier declassification by American psychiatry.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: South African activists launch the National Coalition for Gay and Lesbian Equality
description: The National Coalition for Gay and Lesbian Equality formed in South Africa in 1994 as a national umbrella body. It went on to defend the sexual-orientation clause in the constitution and to litigate key equality cases.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Canada grants refugee status to people persecuted for their sexual orientation
description: Canada began granting refugee status to homosexuals who feared for their safety in their home countries, recognizing persecution based on sexual orientation as grounds for protection. The source timeline lists this under 1994.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The UN Human Rights Committee rules against Tasmania's sodomy laws in Toonen v. Australia
description: In 1994 the UN Human Rights Committee decided Toonen v. Australia, finding that Tasmania's criminalization of consensual sex between men violated the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights. It was the first time a UN body held that sexual-orientation discrimination breached international human-rights law.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The US recognizes fear of anti-gay persecution as grounds for asylum
description: In 1994 the United States recognized fear of persecution based on sexual orientation as a valid basis for asylum, opening protection to LGBTQ people fleeing hostile countries.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Rodney Wilson launches the world's first LGBTQ History Month
description: Missouri teacher Rodney Charles Wilson initiated the first LGBTQ History Month in the United States in October 1994, creating an annual observance dedicated to queer history that later spread to other countries.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Deborah Batts becomes the first openly gay US federal judge
description: Deborah Batts was appointed to the US District Court in New York in 1994, making her the first openly gay or lesbian federal judge in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Queer Asian American groups join San Francisco's Chinese New Year Parade
description: The Gay Asian Pacific Alliance and Asian Pacific Sister marched in San Francisco's Chinese New Year Parade in 1994 — the first public participation of openly queer Asian American communities in an ethnic community event of this kind.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Tokyo holds its first gay pride parade
description: A gay pride parade took place in Tokyo in August 1994, the first march of its kind in Japan.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Wilson Cruz becomes the first gay actor in a leading openly gay TV role
description: In 1994 Wilson Cruz played Rickie Vasquez on My So-Called Life, becoming the first openly gay actor to portray an openly gay character in a leading role on a television series.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Steve Gunderson is outed on the House floor
description: US Representative Steve Gunderson was outed as gay on the House floor in 1994 by Representative Bob Dornan during a debate on federal funding for gay-friendly school curricula. The outing made him one of the first openly gay members of Congress and the first openly gay Republican representative.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Brookside airs the UK's first pre-watershed lesbian kiss
description: The Liverpool-set soap opera Brookside broadcast the first lesbian kiss shown on British television before the watershed in 1994, a widely discussed moment in UK media history.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Pedro Zamora's commitment ceremony airs as the first same-sex ceremony on television
description: MTV's The Real World broadcast the commitment ceremony of Pedro Zamora and Sean Sasser in 1994, showing the couple exchanging vows. It was the first same-sex ceremony shown on television, a milestone in the medium's history.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Sweden's registered partnership law takes effect
description: Sweden's registered partnership law for same-sex couples, passed in 1994, came into force at the start of 1995 and included adoption-related rights. Registered partnership was replaced by full marriage equality in 2009.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Newfoundland and Labrador bans discrimination based on sexual orientation
description: The Canadian province of Newfoundland and Labrador added sexual orientation to its human-rights protections in 1995.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Albania decriminalizes same-sex acts
description: Albania repealed its criminal ban on consensual same-sex acts in 1995, ending the prohibition inherited from the communist-era penal code.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Moldova decriminalizes same-sex acts
description: Moldova removed the criminal ban on consensual sex between men from its penal code in 1995.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Triple combination therapy proves effective against HIV
description: In 1995 researchers showed that triple combination therapy using drugs such as 3TC, AZT and ddC could effectively treat HIV. The breakthrough transformed AIDS from a near-certain death sentence into a manageable condition for many patients.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Gay Advice Darlington/Durham forms in north-east England
description: Local gay and bisexual men set up Gay Advice Darlington/Durham in 1995. The group grew into a charity serving the LGBT community of County Durham and Darlington.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Human Rights Campaign broadens its name and mission
description: In 1995 the Human Rights Campaign dropped the word Fund from its name and expanded its mission statement to advocate equality for gay, lesbian, bisexual and transgender Americans at home, at work and in every community.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Rachel Maddow wins a Rhodes Scholarship as the first openly gay American recipient
description: Rachel Maddow received a Rhodes Scholarship in 1995, becoming the first openly gay or lesbian American to win the international award.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Kings Cross Steelers form as the world's first gay rugby club
description: The Kings Cross Steelers were established in London in 1995 as the first rugby club in the world founded by and for gay men, opening team sport to openly gay players.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Rabbi Margaret Wenig publishes the first Jewish case for gay civil marriage
description: Rabbi Margaret Wenig's essay Truly Welcoming Lesbian and Gay Jews appeared in 1995 in a volume honoring Rabbi Alexander M. Schindler. It was the first published argument addressed to the Jewish community in favor of civil marriage for gay couples.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Ed Flanagan becomes the first openly gay statewide-elected official in the US
description: Vermont State Auditor Ed Flanagan came out as gay in 1995, making him the first openly gay official elected statewide in the United States. He won reelection in 1996 and served through 2001.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Iceland enacts registered partnerships for same-sex couples
description: Iceland passed its registered partnership law in 1996 and it came into force the same year, including step-adoption rights. Joint adoption followed in 2006, and the scheme gave way to full marriage equality in 2010.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Hungary recognizes unregistered cohabitation for same-sex couples
description: Hungary extended unregistered-cohabitation recognition to same-sex couples in 1996, granting them common-law partner status. The arrangement was replaced by registered partnerships in 2009.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The US enacts the Defense of Marriage Act
description: The United States enacted the federal Defense of Marriage Act in 1996, defining marriage as between a man and a woman for federal purposes and allowing states to refuse recognition of same-sex marriages performed elsewhere. Core provisions were struck down in 2013 and 2015.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Burkina Faso equalizes its age of consent
description: Burkina Faso set an equal age of consent for same-sex and different-sex sexual activity in 1996 with the adoption of its new penal code.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)
